mnemonics.title: LADY STAR
mnemonics.content: LADY STAR - LADY = Load Accumulator Direct (LDA), STAR = Store Transfer Accumulator Register (STA). Remember: LOAD puts data IN, STORE takes data OUT.

mnemonics.title: ADD SUBtract MULtiply DIVide
mnemonics.content: ASMD - Basic arithmetic operations: ADD (sum), SUB (difference), MUL (product), DIV (quotient). Think: A S M D in alphabetical order.

mnemonics.title: MDR MAR PCA IR
mnemonics.content: MDR MAR PCA IR - Memory Data Register holds DATA, Memory Address Register holds ADDRESS, Program Counter holds NEXT address, Instruction Register holds INSTRUCTION. Order: MAR→PC→IR→MDR for data flow.

mnemonics.title: INputs OUTputs Data
mnemonics.content: IN takes data INto CPU from input device, OUT sends data OUT from CPU to output device. Remember: IN→CPU, CPU→OUT.

mnemonics.title: Fetch Decode Execute
mnemonics.content: FDE Cycle - Fetch (get instruction), Decode (understand instruction), Execute (perform action). Like: Find→Think→Do.

acronyms.usage: Load data from memory directly into accumulator. Used when data is at a specific memory address.
Store contents of accumulator into a specific memory location.
Holds the memory address from which data will be fetched or to which data will be stored.
Acts as a buffer holding the data that is being transferred to or from memory.
Holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched from memory.
Holds the instruction that is currently being decoded and executed.
Primary register used for arithmetic and logic operations in many architectures.
Instruction to read data from an input device into the CPU/memory.
Instruction to send data from CPU/memory to an output device.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a factory assembly line: FETCH = delivery truck bringing raw materials (instruction), DECODE = foreman reading the work order, EXECUTE = workers processing the materials. Each cycle completes one job.
Picture an inverted triangle: Top (smallest, fastest) = Registers; Middle = Cache; Bottom (largest, slowest) = Main Memory. Data flows up and down like water in a fountain.
Think of registers as desks in an office: MAR is the address book, MDR is the temporary tray, PC is the calendar showing next meeting, IR is the current task list, ACC is the main working desk.
Imagine a reception desk: IN = someone handing you a letter (input), OUT = you giving information to a visitor (output). The CPU is you at your desk processing the information.
Draw a house with a specific street address written on it. The instruction contains this exact address, like having the house number directly written on a letter.

analogies.analogy: Main memory is like a library with millions of books (data). Each book has a unique shelf number (address). LOAD is like borrowing a book to read at your desk (accumulator). STORE is like returning the book to its shelf.
The accumulator is like the display on a calculator - it's where you see and manipulate numbers during calculations. You bring numbers in, do operations, and the result stays on display.

Input devices are like receptionists bringing messages to your desk. Output devices are like assistants taking your completed work to deliver to others. The CPU (you) processes information in between.
Following a recipe: FETCH = reading the next step, DECODE = understanding what the step means, EXECUTE = actually cooking/measuring/mixing. You move through the recipe (PC) step by step.
Data moving between memory and CPU is like vehicles on a highway. The data bus width determines how many lanes (bits) the highway has - more lanes = faster traffic (more data at once).
